title: What affects the rate of a reaction — temperature, concentration, surface area and catalysts
examTip: Rate = quantity of reactant used (or product formed) ÷ time. Always link factor changes back to collision frequency or activation energy.
introTips: Rate of reaction is all about how FAST a reaction goes — not whether it happens.
Every factor links back to one idea: more frequent collisions, or more energetic ones.
Catalysts are a favourite exam topic — make sure you know what they do AND what they don't do.
intro: Why does milk go off faster in summer than in winter? Why do tablets dissolve faster in hot water? Why do factories crush their solid reactants into powder? All of these come down to rate of reaction — how quickly a chemical change happens. Understanding what controls speed in chemistry gives you insight into everything from food preservation to industrial manufacturing.

keyPoints.heading: What rate of reaction means
keyPoints.content: The rate of reaction tells you how quickly reactants are converted into products. You can measure it by tracking how fast reactants are used up OR how fast products are formed. Common measuring methods include: collecting gas in a syringe (volume over time), measuring mass lost as gas escapes from a flask on a balance, watching a colour change using colorimetry, timing how long it takes for a precipitate to form so a cross under a flask disappears, or tracking pH change. The formula is: rate = quantity of reactant used (or product formed) ÷ time. Units are typically mol/s, g/s, or cm³/s depending on what you measure.
writeBullets: Rate of reaction — how quickly reactants are converted into products
Can be measured by how fast reactants disappear OR products appear
Gas syringe method — collect gas produced, measure volume at regular time intervals
Mass loss method — gas escapes from flask on balance; mass decreases over time
Precipitation / cross method — time for cloudiness to hide a cross drawn under the flask
Rate = quantity ÷ time (units: mol/s, g/s, or cm³/s)

keyTerms.def: How quickly reactants are converted into products; measured as quantity of reactant used or product formed per unit time.
A technique that measures how much light is absorbed by a coloured solution, used to track concentration changes.

keyPoints.memTip: RATE = quantity ÷ time — think of speed cameras measuring how fast you go.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse 'measuring rate' with 'the rate itself'. Emphasise that you choose a measurable quantity (volume, mass, colour) and track it over time.

keyPoints.heading: Temperature
keyPoints.content: Increasing temperature speeds up a reaction. When temperature rises, particles move faster — they have more kinetic energy. This means: collisions happen more frequently, AND more of those collisions have enough energy to overcome the activation energy (the minimum energy needed to break bonds and start the reaction). A useful rule of thumb: raising temperature by 10°C approximately doubles the rate. The reverse is also true — cooling slows reactions down. This is why food is kept in a fridge (slows microbial reactions) and why the body's reactions slow dangerously in hypothermia.
writeBullets: Higher temperature → particles move faster → more frequent collisions
More collisions also have energy ≥ activation energy → more effective collisions
Activation energy — the minimum energy particles need to react when they collide
Rule of thumb: +10°C approximately doubles the rate of reaction
Cooling slows reactions — used in food preservation (fridge) and medicine

keyTerms.def: The minimum energy that colliding particles must have for a reaction to occur.
The energy particles have due to their movement; higher temperature means higher average kinetic energy.

keyPoints.memTip: Think of particles as bumper cars — hotter = faster cars = more crashes = more damage (reactions).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Emphasise BOTH effects of temperature: more frequent collisions AND more collisions exceed Ea. Exam questions often want both.

keyPoints.heading: Concentration and pressure
keyPoints.content: Increasing the concentration of a solution (or the pressure of a gas) speeds up a reaction. Higher concentration means more particles are dissolved in the same volume — so particles are closer together and collide more often. Doubling the concentration approximately doubles the collision frequency. For gases, increasing pressure is equivalent: you are squeezing the same number of gas particles into a smaller volume, which increases their concentration and causes more frequent collisions. Example: rusting speeds up in salty water because increased ion concentration accelerates the electrochemical reaction. Pressure only applies to reactions involving gases.
writeBullets: Higher concentration → more particles per unit volume → more frequent collisions → faster rate
Doubling concentration approximately doubles collision frequency
Higher gas pressure → gas particles compressed into smaller volume → greater concentration → more collisions
Concentration effect applies to solutions; pressure effect applies to gases only
Example: salty water speeds up rusting (higher ion concentration)

keyTerms.def: The amount of solute dissolved per unit volume of solution (mol/dm³); higher concentration means more particles in the same space.
For gases, increasing pressure squeezes particles into a smaller volume, increasing their effective concentration.

keyPoints.memTip: More particles, smaller space = more crashes. Concentration and pressure both do this.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students sometimes apply pressure to solid/liquid reactions — remind them pressure only affects gases.

keyPoints.heading: Surface area
keyPoints.content: Breaking a solid into smaller pieces increases the surface area exposed to the other reactant. More surface area means more particles are available to collide with particles in solution or gas. The total mass of solid is unchanged — but the rate increases significantly. A classic example: powdered marble reacts much faster with hydrochloric acid than marble chips of the same mass, because the powder has a much greater surface area. This principle is critical in industry: solid catalysts are often made into pellets or fine powders to maximise surface area. A hazard example: fine flour dust in mills can cause dust explosions because the huge surface area of tiny particles makes combustion extremely rapid.
writeBullets: Smaller pieces → larger surface area exposed to reactant → more particles available to collide
Total mass of solid stays the same — only surface area changes
Powdered marble reacts faster with acid than marble chips of equal mass
Industrial catalysts made into pellets/powder to maximise surface area
Dust explosions: finely divided particles (flour, coal dust) have huge surface area — very fast combustion

keyTerms.def: The total area of a solid exposed to a reactant; increased by cutting or grinding into smaller pieces.

keyPoints.memTip: Chop a block of cheese into cubes — the total cheese is the same but far more surface is exposed.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Surface area questions in exams often use marble + HCl. Make sure students can describe the experiment and explain results in terms of particle collisions.

keyPoints.heading: Catalysts
keyPoints.content: A catalyst increases the rate of reaction without being used up. Because it is not consumed, a catalyst can be reused. Catalysts work by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy. This means more particles have enough energy to react at any given temperature → more effective collisions → faster rate. Two types: homogeneous catalysts are in the same phase as the reactants (e.g., acid catalyst dissolved in solution for esterification); heterogeneous catalysts are in a different phase (e.g., solid iron catalyst used in the Haber process for nitrogen and hydrogen gases). Industrial importance: catalysts allow reactions to run at lower temperatures, saving energy and reducing costs — more sustainable. Enzymes are biological catalysts — e.g., amylase breaks down starch. Each enzyme has an active site that is specific to one substrate.
writeBullets: Catalyst — increases reaction rate without being used up; can be reused
Provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy
Lower Ea → more particles have enough energy to react → faster rate
Homogeneous catalyst — same phase as reactants (e.g., dissolved acid)
Heterogeneous catalyst — different phase from reactants (e.g., solid iron in Haber process)
Enzymes are biological catalysts — specific to one substrate via active site

keyTerms.def: A substance that increases the rate of a reaction by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy, without being used up.
A catalyst in the same physical phase (state) as the reactants.
A catalyst in a different physical phase from the reactants, typically a solid used with gas or liquid reactants.
A biological catalyst — a protein with an active site specific to one substrate.

keyPoints.memTip: Catalyst = shortcut through the mountain (lower Ea), not a bigger engine (no higher temperature).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Exam favourite: 'explain how a catalyst increases rate'. Students must say LOWER ACTIVATION ENERGY and more particles exceed it — not just 'lowers energy'.

tables.title: Factors affecting rate of reaction
rows: Particles move faster; more frequent collisions; more collisions exceed activation energy
Reactions in the body slow in hypothermia; hot water dissolves tablets faster
More particles per unit volume; collisions more frequent
Higher concentration of acid reacts faster with magnesium
Gas particles compressed into smaller volume; more frequent collisions
Higher pressure speeds up ammonia synthesis in Haber process
More particles exposed at surface; more available to collide
Powdered marble reacts faster with HCl than marble chips
Provides lower activation energy pathway; more particles have enough energy
Iron catalyst in Haber process; platinum in catalytic converters

0.question: A student measures the rate of a reaction by timing how long it takes for a cross drawn under a flask to disappear. What is forming in the flask?
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. The cross disappears because a precipitate forms and makes the solution cloudy — a classic AQA rate experiment.
0.wrongFeedback: Think about what would make a clear solution turn cloudy so you can no longer see a cross underneath — it must be a solid precipitate forming.

2.question: Why does increasing the pressure of a gas speed up a reaction?
options: Gas particles are pushed into a smaller volume, increasing their concentration and making collisions more frequent
Gas particles gain more kinetic energy and move faster
The gas becomes more concentrated because new particles are added
2.correctFeedback: Spot on. Pressure squeezes the same number of particles into a smaller space — effectively increasing concentration — so collisions happen more often.
2.wrongFeedback: Pressure alone doesn't change particle speed or activation energy. The key is that the same particles are in a smaller volume — closer together, so they collide more often.

4.question: A catalyst increases the rate of reaction. What does it do to the activation energy?
options: It lowers the activation energy by providing an alternative reaction pathway
It raises the activation energy so more particles can overcome it
It gives particles more kinetic energy so they move faster
It has no effect on activation energy — it just increases collision frequency
4.correctFeedback: Perfect. A catalyst provides an alternative lower-energy pathway — the activation energy is lower, so more particles have enough energy to react.
4.wrongFeedback: Remember: a catalyst doesn't change particle energies or collision frequency directly. Its key action is lowering the activation energy by offering a different reaction route.

commonMistakes: Saying a catalyst 'gives particles more energy' — catalysts lower activation energy, they do not heat up the particles.
Forgetting to mention BOTH effects of temperature: more frequent collisions AND more collisions exceeding the activation energy.
Applying pressure to reactions that don't involve gases — pressure only affects the rate when reactants or products are gases.
Confusing 'mass of solid' with 'surface area' — grinding up a solid doesn't change the mass, only the surface area exposed.
Writing that a catalyst is 'used up' in the reaction — catalysts are not consumed and can be reused.
examTips: When asked to explain the effect of any factor, always link it to collision frequency and/or the proportion of collisions that exceed the activation energy.
For catalyst questions: state that it provides an alternative pathway with LOWER activation energy, so MORE particles can react.
Measurement method questions often appear — know the cross/precipitate method, gas syringe, and mass loss method and be able to describe each.
revisionCardBullets: Rate of reaction = quantity of reactant used (or product formed) ÷ time
Measured by: gas volume, mass loss, cross disappearing (precipitation), colorimetry
Higher temperature → faster particles → more frequent collisions + more exceed Ea → faster rate
Rule of thumb: +10°C ≈ doubles the rate
Higher concentration / pressure → more particles per volume → more frequent collisions
Smaller solid pieces → greater surface area → more particles exposed → faster rate
Catalyst → lower activation energy pathway → more particles can react → faster rate
Catalyst is NOT used up — it can be reused
Homogeneous catalyst: same phase as reactants; heterogeneous: different phase
Enzymes are biological catalysts with an active site specific to one substrate
Activation energy = minimum energy particles need to react on collision
Dust explosions occur because finely divided particles have huge surface area
